findings.description: Reflected XSS in SampleAjaxPlugin - HTTP request data (actionName from URL path, params from request parameter) written directly to response output without HTML encoding
findings.reasoning: The SampleAjaxPlugin.service() method receives actionName (extracted from the URL path via AjaxUtil.getNextPathPart) and params (from request.getParameter("params")) and writes them directly to response output without any HTML encoding. The actionName comes from req.getRequestURI() and the params come from request.getParameter("params") - both are attacker-controlled. No encoding is applied before writing to the response writer. This is a direct reflected XSS vulnerability. The WikiAjaxDispatcherServlet at line 136 reads request.getParameter("params") and passes it to the servlet's service() method at line 147.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: HTTP request URI -> AjaxUtil.getNextPathPart -> actionName -> response.getWriter().print() without encoding. Also: request.getParameter('params') -> paramValues -> response.getWriter().print() without encoding.
evidence.notes: Both actionName (from URL path) and params (from query parameter) are written directly to response output without any HTML encoding. Content-type not explicitly set to prevent HTML rendering.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
GET /wiki/ajax/SampleAjaxPlugin/<script>alert(1)</script> HTTP/1.1
Host: target.example.com

# Or via params parameter:
GET /wiki/ajax/SampleAjaxPlugin/ajaxAction?params=<script>alert(document.cookie)</script> HTTP/1.1
Host: target.example.com
exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker crafts a URL with XSS payload in the path or params parameter that gets reflected back in the response without encoding
exploit_payload.prerequisites: The wiki must have the SampleAjaxPlugin registered and accessible (it is included by default)

findings.description: Stored XSS in CookieTag - cookie value read from HTTP cookie and written to JSP page output without HTML encoding
findings.reasoning: The CookieTag reads a cookie value (either the whole cookie value or a specific item from the cookie), then writes it directly to the JSP page output via pageContext.getOut().print(out) without any HTML encoding. The cookie value is attacker-controlled - an attacker can set the cookie value to contain HTML/JavaScript, and when the CookieTag renders the page, the malicious content will be reflected unescaped into the HTML. The variable 'out' is set from cookie.getValue() or getItemValue() which parses the cookie string, both of which return attacker-controlled content without encoding.

evidence.code_context:
CookieTag.doEndTag() lines 218-260:
- out = getItemValue(cookie, m_item); // OR
- out = cookie.getValue();
...
pageContext.getOut().print(out); // Line 256 - no encoding applied

getItemValue() calls parseCookieValues() which parses cookie string via split and URLDecoder.decode - no HTML encoding.
cookie.getValue() returns raw cookie value - no HTML encoding.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: HTTP Cookie header -> cookie.getValue()/getItemValue() -> out variable -> pageContext.getOut().print(out) without HTML encoding
evidence.notes: The cookie value is completely attacker-controlled. If an attacker can set the cookie (e.g., via CSRF or subdomain attack), they can inject HTML/JS that gets rendered when this tag is used on any wiki page.

exploit_payload.code_snippet: # Set malicious cookie value via JavaScript or direct browser manipulation: document.cookie = 'wiki_prefs=; path=/'; # Or via subdomain cookie injection: # Set cookie on .example.com that is sent to wiki.example.com # When a JSP page uses , the XSS fires
exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker sets a cookie containing HTML/JavaScript payload; when a wiki page containing the CookieTag renders the cookie value, the XSS executes in victim's browser
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must be able to set the target cookie value (possible via subdomain takeover, CSRF, or direct browser access)

findings.description: XSS in FeedDiscoveryTag - wiki variable/RSS URL written to HTML attribute in response without adequate encoding, allowing injection into HTML link tags
findings.code_snippet: pageContext.getOut().print("<link rel=\"alternate\" type=\"application/rss+xml\" title=\"RSS wiki feed for the entire site.\" href=\""+rssURL+"\" />\n");
findings.reasoning: The FeedDiscoveryTag writes rssURL directly into an HTML href attribute without proper encoding. The rssURL comes from engine.getGlobalRSSURL(), and the siteName comes from Feed.getSiteName() which is derived from wiki configuration variables (which can be user-controlled from WikiContext). While siteName is HTML-encoded with TextUtil.replaceEntities(), the rssURL itself is NOT encoded before being placed inside the href attribute. If an attacker can control the RSS URL (e.g., via wiki configuration), they can inject javascript: protocol or close the attribute and add event handlers.

evidence.code_context:
FeedDiscoveryTag lines 55-59:
String siteName = Feed.getSiteName(m_wikiContext);
siteName = TextUtil.replaceEntities(siteName); // siteName IS encoded

pageContext.getOut().print("<link ... href=\""+rssURL+"\" />\n"); // rssURL NOT encoded

rssURL comes from engine.getGlobalRSSURL() - derived from wiki config/variables.
rssFeedURL also not encoded at lines 72-73 and 75-76.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: WikiContext (request-derived) -> VariableManager.getValue() -> Feed.getSiteName() -> rssURL -> pageContext.getOut().print() with rssURL unencoded in href attribute
evidence.notes: siteName IS encoded but rssURL is not. rssURL injection in an href context could enable javascript: URL injection.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
# If attacker can control wiki engine RSS URL setting (via wiki admin config):
# Set RSS URL to: javascript:alert(document.cookie)
# Or inject via href attribute break:
# rssURL = '" onmouseover="alert(1)" x="'
# Results in: <link href="" onmouseover="alert(1)" x="" />
exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker with wiki configuration access sets RSS URL to malicious value that gets reflected unencoded into HTML link tag href attribute
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker needs control over wiki RSS URL configuration
exploit_payload.category: XSS via URL injection in HTML attribute

findings.description: XSS in FeedDiscoveryTag - blog feed URLs written directly to HTML link tag attributes without encoding
findings.reasoning: Similar to line 59, blogFeedURL and atomFeedURL are constructed from engine.getURL() with page name embedded, and written directly into HTML href attributes without encoding. The page name comes from the wiki page context which can be influenced by user input (wiki page names). If a page name contains characters like quote or angle bracket, it could break out of the attribute context and inject HTML/JavaScript.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: WikiContext/page name -> engine.encodeName() -> engine.getURL() -> blogFeedURL/atomFeedURL -> print() without HTML encoding in href attribute
evidence.notes: The encodedName is URL-encoded but not HTML attribute-encoded. A specially crafted page name with quotes could break out of the href attribute.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
# Create a wiki page with a name containing HTML injection:
# Page name: MyBlog" onload="alert(1)
# Results in URL parameter: page=MyBlog%22+onload%3D%22alert(1)
# If engine.getURL doesn't HTML-encode quotes, results in:
# <link href="rss.jsp?page=MyBlog" onload="alert(1)" />
exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker creates a wiki page with a specially crafted name containing HTML injection that gets reflected unencoded in the blog feed URL attribute
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must be able to create wiki pages with special characters in the name
exploit_payload.category: XSS via URL injection in HTML attribute

findings.description: XSS in CalendarTag - query string from HTTP request reflected unencoded into HTML calendar navigation links
findings.code_snippet: out.write( ""+ getMonthNaviLink(prevCal,"<<", queryString)+ ""+ getMonthLink( cal )+ ""+ getMonthNaviLink(nextCal,">>", queryString)+ "\n" );
findings.reasoning: The CalendarTag reads the query string from the HTTP request via engine.safeGetQueryString(httpServletRequest) and passes it to getMonthNaviLink(). Inside getMonthNaviLink(), the queryString is processed with TextUtil.replaceEntities() but only after extracting and removing specific parts. The processed query string is then appended to the generated URL. However, if the encoding is insufficient or bypassed (e.g., if the queryString contains characters that can break out of the href attribute context), this could lead to XSS. The codeflow shows the HTTP request's query string flows through TextUtil into the output.

evidence.code_context: CalendarTag lines 318-326: HttpServletRequest httpServletRequest = m_wikiContext.getHttpRequest(); String queryString = engine.safeGetQueryString(httpServletRequest); out.write(""+getMonthNaviLink(prevCal,"<<", queryString)+...) In getMonthNaviLink (lines 213-268): queryString = TextUtil.replaceEntities(queryString); // HTML entity encoding applied here ... url = url + "&"+queryString; ... result = ""+txt+""; // queryString embedded in URL TextUtil.replaceEntities() does apply HTML encoding, which may mitigate some attacks but URL-specific attacks (javascript:) might still be possible via manipulating the URL construction.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: HttpServletRequest.getQueryString() -> safeGetQueryString() -> CalendarTag.doWikiStartTag() -> getMonthNaviLink() -> TextUtil.replaceEntities() -> URL construction -> out.write()
evidence.notes: TextUtil.replaceEntities() does provide HTML encoding. The risk depends on whether replaceEntities is sufficient for URL context and whether it properly encodes all dangerous characters. The codeflow path in CodeQL does pass through TextUtil which could be a sanitizer.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
# Attacker crafts a URL with malicious query string to the wiki calendar page:
GET /wiki/SomePage?calendar.date=010123&"><script>alert(1)</script>= HTTP/1.1
Host: target.example.com
# If TextUtil.replaceEntities() doesn't fully sanitize, the injected script could execute
exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker crafts a URL with an XSS payload in the query string that gets reflected in calendar navigation links when a page uses the CalendarTag
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Wiki page must use the CalendarTag plugin; attacker sends crafted link to victim
exploit_payload.category: Reflected XSS via query string injection

findings.description: BugReportHandler plugin output - user-controlled params written to StringWriter for wiki page creation, not directly to HTTP response
findings.reasoning: The BugReportHandler writes user-controlled parameters (title, description, version, other params) to a StringWriter via PrintWriter. This creates wiki markup content that is then saved as a wiki page. The content is not directly written to the HTTP response body - it goes through the wiki rendering pipeline which should handle XSS through proper encoding. The code uses StringWriter/PrintWriter pattern (str/out variables), not response output. Looking at the execute() method, it writes wiki markup to a StringWriter and the result would be saved as wiki content. This is a false positive as the output goes to a StringWriter for wiki page creation, not directly to the HTTP response.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: The CodeQL path shows user input flowing through DefaultPluginManager (line 342) to PluginContent, but the actual data at lines 116-121 goes to a StringWriter, not directly to the HTTP response.
evidence.notes: This appears to be a false positive - the data is written to a StringWriter for wiki page content creation, not directly to HTTP response output.

findings.description: AuthorTag - author name written to output but goes through wiki rendering pipeline (MarkupParser + RenderingManager.getHTML) which handles encoding
findings.reasoning: The AuthorTag processes the author name through the wiki rendering pipeline (MarkupParser + RenderingManager.getHTML) before printing it. The author variable at line 72 is the result of mgr.getHTML(m_wikiContext, d) where d is the parsed wiki document. The rendering manager converts wiki markup to HTML and properly handles encoding during this process. While the author name comes from wiki page metadata (which could be user-controlled), the wiki rendering engine applies proper HTML encoding during the conversion from wiki markup to HTML. This path through the rendering engine acts as a sanitizer. This is likely a false positive.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: WikiContext -> WeblogPlugin -> DefaultPluginManager -> PluginContent -> CleanTextRenderer/CreoleRenderer -> RenderingManager -> AuthorTag: the data passes through the wiki rendering engine which provides encoding.
evidence.notes: The wiki rendering pipeline (MarkupParser + RenderingManager.getHTML) acts as a sanitizer. This appears to be a false positive.

findings.description: VariableTag - wiki variable value written to output but goes through TextUtil.replaceEntities() which provides HTML encoding
findings.reasoning: The VariableTag explicitly calls TextUtil.replaceEntities(value) before writing to the output. This HTML-encodes the value, converting dangerous characters like <, >, &, and " into their HTML entity equivalents. Even though the variable value comes from user-controlled wiki variables (via VariableManager.getValue), the HTML encoding applied by TextUtil.replaceEntities() prevents XSS. This is a false positive - the code correctly sanitizes the output.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: WikiContext -> VariableManager.getValue() -> value variable -> TextUtil.replaceEntities() -> out.write() - the replaceEntities call is the sanitizer
evidence.notes: This is a false positive. TextUtil.replaceEntities() explicitly encodes HTML special characters. CodeQL may have missed this sanitizer.

findings.description: PluginTag - plugin output written to JSP page output but goes through plugin execution pipeline (DefaultPluginManager.execute) which returns pre-rendered HTML
findings.reasoning: The PluginTag writes the result of executePlugin() to the JSP output. The executePlugin() method calls DefaultPluginManager.execute() which runs the wiki plugin and returns HTML string. Plugin output is intended to be HTML - plugins generate HTML markup as their output. The data flowing here is wiki plugin HTML output that has already been processed/rendered. The source of the taint (WikiContext/WeblogPlugin) goes through the wiki plugin execution pipeline. The plugin itself is responsible for encoding its output. While individual plugins may have XSS issues (like BugReportHandler), this PluginTag itself is not the direct cause. This is a false positive at this specific location - the issue would be in each individual plugin's execute() method.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: WikiContext -> WeblogPlugin.execute() -> DefaultPluginManager.execute() -> PluginTag.executePlugin() -> pageContext.getOut().write(). The plugin execution pipeline is the intended mechanism for generating HTML.
evidence.notes: False positive at this location - the PluginTag is a generic dispatcher, XSS issues would be in specific plugin implementations.

findings.description: PluginTag.doAfterBody - same as doEndTag, plugin output written via getPreviousOut().write() - false positive as plugin output is designed to be HTML
findings.code_snippet: getPreviousOut().write( executePlugin( m_plugin, m_args, (bc != null) ? bc.getString() : null) );
findings.reasoning: Same as line 126 - this is the doAfterBody() variant. Plugin output is intended to be HTML markup. The XSS risk lies in individual plugin implementations, not in the PluginTag dispatcher itself. This is a false positive at this specific location.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: Same dataflow path as line 126 - wiki plugin execution pipeline.
evidence.notes: False positive - same reasoning as line 126 finding.

findings.description: TranslateTag - wiki text processed through engine.textToHTML() rendering pipeline before output - false positive as rendering engine handles encoding
findings.reasoning: TranslateTag takes wiki markup text from the JSP body content and processes it through context.getEngine().textToHTML(context, wikiText) before writing to output. This is the WikiEngine's wiki-to-HTML conversion pipeline which handles encoding. The result is rendered HTML output from the wiki rendering engine. The wikiText comes from the JSP tag body, not directly from HTTP request parameters. This is a false positive.

evidence.notes: False positive - data goes through the wiki rendering engine (textToHTML) which is the intended sanitizer/encoder for wiki content.

findings.description: InsertPageTag - wiki page content written via engine.getHTML() rendering pipeline - false positive as rendering engine handles encoding
findings.reasoning: InsertPageTag renders wiki page content through engine.getHTML() which processes the wiki markup through the full wiki rendering pipeline (CleanTextRenderer/CreoleRenderer via RenderingManager). This is the intended mechanism for inserting wiki page content. The rendering engine handles conversion from wiki markup to HTML with appropriate encoding. This is a false positive - the wiki rendering engine is the sanitizer here.

evidence.notes: False positive - wiki rendering pipeline is the intended encoder. Data flows through CleanTextRenderer/CreoleRenderer as shown in the codeflow.

findings.description: XHtmlElementToWikiTranslator - text content written to output is part of HTML-to-wiki translation tool, not an HTTP response sink
findings.reasoning: The XHtmlElementToWikiTranslator is a utility class for converting HTML to wiki markup. The m_out here is a PrintWriter that writes wiki markup (not HTTP response output). The class is used to convert rich text editor content (HTML) back into wiki syntax for storage. The output is wiki markup text, not an HTTP response body. This is a false positive - the "response output" that CodeQL identifies is not an HTTP servlet response but rather a wiki markup writer. The path through WeblogPlugin/PluginContent/DefaultPluginManager is the source of the HTML content being translated, not a path to HTTP response.

evidence.notes: False positive - m_out is a wiki markup writer, not an HTTP response output stream.

findings.description: CookieTag writes raw cookie value to JSP output without HTML encoding, enabling stored/persistent XSS via cookie injection
findings.reasoning: The CookieTag reads cookie values from the HTTP request (either cookie.getValue() or getItemValue(cookie, m_item) which parses cookie values after URL-decoding) and writes them directly to the JSP page output at line 256 via pageContext.getOut().print(out) without any HTML encoding. The cookie value is URL-decoded (via URLDecoder.decode()) but not HTML-encoded before being written to the response. This means an attacker who can set a malicious cookie value can inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript into the page. The code flow from the data flow analysis confirms: cookie value from findCookie() → getItemValue() or cookie.getValue() → out variable → pageContext.getOut().print(out) at line 256, with no HTML encoding applied.

evidence.code_context: Line 241: out = getItemValue(cookie, m_item) OR out = cookie.getValue(); Line 256: pageContext.getOut().print(out); The getItemValue() method reads the cookie value, splits it and URL-decodes it via parseCookieValues/decode which uses URLDecoder.decode, but does NOT HTML-encode the result before returning it. The decoded value flows directly to print() at line 256.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: CookieTag.java:241 -> CookieTag.java:256 direct flow via cookie.getValue() or getItemValue(). CookieTag.java:316-345 -> CookieTag.java:256 via parseCookieValues + decode + getItemValue flow. No HTML encoding applied in this path.
evidence.notes: URL-decoding is performed via URLDecoder.decode() but HTML encoding via TextUtil.replaceEntities() or similar is NOT applied before writing to pageContext.getOut().print()

exploit_payload.scenario: An attacker who can set or control a cookie value can inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript that executes when the wiki page renders the CookieTag
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must be able to set or manipulate cookie values for the wiki domain, which can be achieved via subdomain cookie injection or another XSS vector

findings.description: AuthorTag writes rendered HTML from wiki markup engine without additional encoding after initial HTML-entity encoding of author name
findings.reasoning: The AuthorTag has a dual code path: (1) If the author's page doesn't exist, the author name is HTML-encoded via TextUtil.replaceEntities(author) and written directly - this is safe. (2) If the author's page exists, the code builds a wiki link '[author|author]', renders it through the full wiki markup renderer (mgr.getHTML()), and writes the resulting HTML directly without any additional encoding. The security depends entirely on whether the RenderingManager properly sanitizes output. The author name comes from page.getAuthor() which is stored page metadata - if an attacker can create a page with a specially crafted author name containing wiki markup that the renderer converts to dangerous HTML, XSS could occur. The code flow from CodeQL traces this through WeblogPlugin and RenderingManager. This is a manual review case because exploitability depends on whether: (a) the renderer itself properly sanitizes, (b) page authors are user-controlled.

evidence.code_context:
AuthorTag.java line 57: author = TextUtil.replaceEntities(author); // HTML encodes
AuthorTag.java line 60: if(engine.pageExists(author)) { // IF page exists
AuthorTag.java line 64: MarkupParser p = mgr.getParser(m_wikiContext, '['+author+'|'+author+']');
AuthorTag.java line 66: WikiDocument d = p.parse();
AuthorTag.java line 68: author = mgr.getHTML(m_wikiContext, d); // renders to HTML
AuthorTag.java line 72: pageContext.getOut().print(author); // written raw

Note: author was HTML-encoded before being used in wiki link, but mgr.getHTML() returns rendered HTML that is written without further encoding
evidence.dataflow_analysis: WikiContext.java:503 -> WikiContext.java:506 -> WeblogPlugin.java:215 -> WeblogPlugin.java:244 -> DefaultPluginManager.java:342 -> PluginContent.java:203 -> PluginContent.java:219 -> PluginContent.java:153 -> PluginContent.java:121 -> RenderingManager.java:271 -> AuthorTag.java:69 -> AuthorTag.java:72
evidence.notes: The author name is HTML-encoded before being used as wiki markup, so direct HTML injection via the author name is unlikely. However, the rendered HTML output from mgr.getHTML() is written raw. The actual XSS risk depends on whether the wiki renderer can generate dangerous HTML from the sanitized author name.

findings.description: UserProfileTag properly HTML-encodes user profile data before writing to JSP output
findings.reasoning: The UserProfileTag at line 202 explicitly calls TextUtil.replaceEntities(result) before writing to the JSP output. TextUtil.replaceEntities() replaces &, <, >, and " with their HTML entity equivalents (&, <, >, "), which is a proper HTML encoding. Despite CodeQL flagging this as a potential XSS (following the data flow of user-controlled profile fields like getLoginName(), getFullname(), getEmail()), the output IS properly HTML-encoded before being written to the response. This is a false positive.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: Even though user profile data (loginName at line 142, fullname at line 146, email at line 154, wikiName at line 166) flows to result which flows to line 202, the TextUtil.replaceEntities() call at the output point provides proper HTML encoding.
evidence.notes: False positive - CodeQL traced the data flow but missed the HTML encoding applied at the output sink.

findings.description: MessagesTag properly HTML-encodes session messages before writing to JSP output
findings.reasoning: MessagesTag at line 105 uses TextUtil.replaceEntities(messages[0]) and at line 113 uses TextUtil.replaceEntities(messages[i]) for each message before appending to the StringBuilder. The final output at line 117 (pageContext.getOut().println(sb.toString())) writes the pre-encoded content. TextUtil.replaceEntities() properly HTML-encodes &, <, >, and " characters. Despite the data flow from WikiSession.getMessages() to the output, each message is properly HTML-encoded before being written. This is a false positive.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: WikiSession.java:339 (getMessages) -> MessagesTag.java:99 -> MessagesTag.java:105 (with replaceEntities applied) or line 113 (with replaceEntities applied) -> MessagesTag.java:117 (output). Encoding is applied at the intermediate step before output.
evidence.notes: False positive - the CodeQL tool traced the data flow but did not recognize TextUtil.replaceEntities() as a sanitizer.

findings.description: VariableTag properly HTML-encodes variable values before writing to JSP output
findings.reasoning: VariableTag at line 107 explicitly calls TextUtil.replaceEntities(value) before writing to the output. TextUtil.replaceEntities() properly HTML-encodes &, <, >, and " characters. Even if the variable value contains user-controlled data, it is properly HTML-encoded before output. This is a false positive.

evidence.code_context:
VariableTag.java line 107: out.write( TextUtil.replaceEntities(value) );

TextUtil.replaceEntities() replaces &, <, >, and " with HTML entities before output, providing proper HTML encoding.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: The value variable flows to TextUtil.replaceEntities() which encodes it before being passed to out.write(). No unencoded output.
evidence.notes: False positive - proper HTML encoding applied at output sink.

findings.description: CalendarTag inserts user-controlled queryString into HTML href attribute after HTML-encoding - URL injection risk in href context
findings.code_snippet: out.write( ""+ getMonthNaviLink(prevCal,"<<", queryString)+ ""+ getMonthLink( cal )+ ""+ getMonthNaviLink(nextCal,">>", queryString)+ "\n" );
findings.reasoning: The CalendarTag reads the queryString from the HTTP request (safeGetQueryString) and passes it to getMonthNaviLink(). Inside getMonthNaviLink(), the queryString is HTML-encoded via TextUtil.replaceEntities() before being appended to a URL in an href attribute. The HTML encoding converts < > " & to entities, which prevents HTML injection. However, the queryString (even after HTML encoding) is appended to a URL in an href attribute context. An attacker could inject a value like 'javascript:alert(1)' which, after HTML encoding, remains 'javascript:alert(1)' (no HTML special chars) and could be injected into the URL. However, the queryString is appended AFTER the base URL (which already contains the page and calendar.date parameters), so the injection would need to add new query parameters. The 'calendar.date' parameter removal logic manipulates the queryString. This warrants manual review to confirm whether javascript: protocol injection is possible in the URL context.

evidence.code_context: CalendarTag.java line 218: queryString = TextUtil.replaceEntities(queryString); // HTML encodes ... CalendarTag.java line 258: url = url + '&' + queryString; // appended to URL CalendarTag.java line 260: result = '' + txt + ''; // written to HTML href The HTML encoding prevents direct HTML injection but does not prevent URL-based attacks if the queryString itself contains URL-special characters that survive HTML encoding (like 'javascript:' scheme).
evidence.dataflow_analysis: HTTP request queryString (safeGetQueryString) -> CalendarTag.java:320 -> getMonthNaviLink() -> TextUtil.replaceEntities() (partial sanitization) -> URL construction -> href attribute -> out.write()
evidence.notes: HTML encoding is applied but the context is a URL within an href attribute. The 'javascript:' protocol does not contain HTML-special characters and would survive replaceEntities(). However, the queryString is appended as a query parameter value (after &), not as the protocol part of the URL, which limits the attack surface considerably.

findings.description: AttachmentServlet sendError with page name - page name comes from wiki context, not raw user input; servlet containers typically encode sendError messages
findings.reasoning: At line 311, the AttachmentServlet sends an error response with a message containing the page name: msg = "Attachment '" + page + "', version " + ver + " does not exist;". The `page` variable comes from `context.getPage().getName()` which is the wiki page name resolved from the request URL path by WikiEngine. While this is ultimately derived from user input (the URL path), it passes through WikiEngine's page resolution logic. Additionally, the `sendError()` method in modern servlet containers (Tomcat, Jetty) typically HTML-encodes the error message string in their default error pages, providing mitigation. The data flow in CodeQL traces from `req.getRequestURI()` at line 203 through to the error message at line 311. However, the page name goes through WikiEngine's context creation and page resolution which may normalize/restrict it. This is likely a false positive given the servlet container protection and the indirect data path.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: AttachmentServlet.java:203 (req.getRequestURI/param) -> AttachmentServlet.java:311 (sendError with page name in msg). The page name passes through WikiEngine.createContext() and page resolution before reaching the error message.
evidence.notes: While there is a data flow path from user input to sendError message, servlet containers typically HTML-encode sendError messages in their default error pages. Also, the page name is processed through wiki engine's page resolution logic.

findings.description: AttachmentServlet sendRedirect with validateNextPage - nextPage parameter is validated before redirect, preventing open redirect/XSS
findings.code_snippet: res.sendRedirect( validateNextPage( nextPage, m_engine.getURL( WikiContext.ERROR, \"\", null, false ) ) );
findings.reasoning: At line 304, the AttachmentServlet calls res.sendRedirect() with the result of validateNextPage(). The validateNextPage() method (at lines 434-448) validates that the nextPage URL either is a relative URL (no "://") or starts with the engine's base URL. If neither condition is met, it redirects to the errorPage instead. This validation prevents open redirect attacks. The nextPage parameter comes from req.getParameter("nextpage") at line 204. While user-controlled, the validateNextPage() sanitization ensures only same-origin URLs are used for redirection. The sendRedirect() call with a validated URL is not an XSS sink - it sets the Location header, not the response body. This is a false positive.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: AttachmentServlet.java:204 (req.getParameter nextpage) -> AttachmentServlet.java:304 (sendRedirect with validateNextPage applied). The validateNextPage() acts as a sanitizer/validator that restricts the redirect target.
evidence.notes: False positive - validateNextPage() properly validates the redirect URL. sendRedirect() sets Location header, not response body, so XSS via this path is not possible in modern browsers.

findings.description: WikiJSPFilter.filter() writes processed response string - this is the full rendered page content, not direct user input injection
findings.reasoning: The WikiJSPFilter intercepts the full JSP response, processes it through insertResources() to inject CSS/JS resource markers, and then writes the modified response back. The string `r` is the result of the JSP rendering pipeline (already rendered HTML from the JSP) plus resource injections from TemplateManager. The filter does not directly inject raw user input into the response - it processes the already-rendered response. The resource strings inserted by insertResources() come from TemplateManager.getResourceRequests() which handles wiki plugin/template resources, not direct user input. The CodeQL false alarm is likely tracing the fact that the full response (which may contain user data rendered by other components) flows through this filter. This is a false positive at this specific location - if there were XSS, it would be at the point where user data was originally rendered into the response (e.g., in specific JSP files or tag handlers), not at this filter level.

evidence.code_context: WikiJSPFilter.java lines 120-133: The filter() method processes the full response string through insertResources() which injects TemplateManager resource requests (CSS/JS files, HTTP headers) into placeholder markers in the response. The final response is written via out.write(r) or response.getWriter().write(r). This is the full rendered page, not direct user input injection.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: The 'r' variable is produced by filter(wikiContext, responseWrapper) which: 1) Takes the full rendered JSP response, 2) Inserts resource strings at marker positions via insertResources(), 3) Returns the modified string. The resources inserted come from TemplateManager, not direct user HTTP input.
evidence.notes: False positive - the filter processes already-rendered content and injects template resources. Any XSS would originate in the components that render user data into the response body (tag handlers, JSP files), not in this filter layer.

findings.description: Stored XSS in BreadcrumbsTag - page name written to HTML without encoding
findings.code_snippet: out.print("<a class=\"" + linkclass + "\" href=\"" + m_wikiContext.getViewURL(curPage)+ "\">" + curPage + "</a>");
findings.reasoning: The variable `curPage` is a wiki page name retrieved from the session-stored breadcrumb trail. Page names can contain characters such as `<`, `>`, `"` which are not HTML-encoded before being written to the JSP output. The page name appears in two places: (1) in the HTML body between the anchor tags, and (2) in the URL. The body context is the dangerous one - if an attacker creates a page with a name like `<script>alert(1)</script>` and that page is visited (adding it to the trail), the script will execute when the breadcrumb trail is rendered. This is effectively stored XSS since the payload is stored in the session breadcrumb trail and re-rendered on every subsequent page view.

evidence.code_context: out.print("" + curPage + ""); The breadcrumb trail stores page names (String) in a session-scoped LinkedList. When rendered, curPage is written directly to HTML body without any HTML encoding. TextUtil.replaceEntities() is NOT called on curPage.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: Source: page = m_wikiContext.getPage().getName() -> trail.pushItem(page) -> curPage = trail.get(i) -> out.print(...curPage...)
evidence.notes: TextUtil.replaceEntities() is used in other tags (UserNameTag, AuthorTag) but is missing here. The beautifyTitle() method also does not HTML-encode.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker creates a wiki page with an XSS payload as its name; when victims browse that page, the malicious name is stored in their breadcrumb trail and executes on every subsequent page load
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must be able to create wiki pages (contributor access)

findings.description: XSS in PageNameTag - page name written to HTML output without encoding via beautifyTitle()
findings.reasoning: The PageNameTag writes the page name (or filename for attachments) directly to the JSP response without HTML encoding. For attachments, `getFileName()` returns the file name unencoded. For regular pages, `beautifyTitle(getName())` returns the page name possibly with added spaces but without HTML entity encoding. Page/attachment names controlled by users can contain `<`, `>`, `"` characters enabling stored XSS when this tag is rendered in an HTML context.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: Source: page.getName() / attachment.getFileName() -> beautifyTitle() (no HTML encoding) -> pageContext.getOut().print()
evidence.notes: beautifyTitle() implementation: if m_beautifyTitle config is enabled, calls TextUtil.beautifyString() which inserts spaces but does NOT HTML-encode. If disabled, returns the title as-is. Neither path applies HTML entity encoding.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker creates a wiki page or uploads an attachment with an XSS payload in the name; the PageNameTag renders it unencoded into the HTML body
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must be able to create wiki pages or upload attachments

findings.description: XSS in ParentPageNameTag - parent page name written to HTML output without encoding via beautifyTitle()
findings.reasoning: ParentPageNameTag writes the parent page name to the JSP response via `beautifyTitle()` which does not apply HTML encoding. For attachments, `getParentName()` returns the parent page name unencoded. For regular pages (blog entries/comments), the name is derived from page.getName() and written without HTML encoding. An attacker who can create a wiki page with an XSS payload in its name can trigger execution when this tag renders the parent page name in any view that shows child pages/attachments.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: Source: page.getName() / attachment.getParentName() -> beautifyTitle() (no HTML encoding) -> pageContext.getOut().print()
evidence.notes: Same pattern as PageNameTag - beautifyTitle() does not apply HTML entity encoding.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker creates a parent wiki page with XSS payload in the name; the ParentPageNameTag renders the unencoded name into the HTML body when viewing child pages or attachments
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must be able to create wiki pages and upload attachments or create child pages

findings.description: XSS in ParentPageNameTag - second location where parent page name written without HTML encoding
findings.reasoning: This is the second output path in ParentPageNameTag for regular (non-attachment) wiki pages. The `name` variable is derived from `page.getName()` after stripping blog/comment suffixes, then passed through `beautifyTitle()` which doesn't apply HTML encoding. An attacker who can create a page with an XSS payload in the name can trigger XSS when this tag renders.

evidence.notes: beautifyTitle() does not HTML-encode. No sanitization applied.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker creates a blog entry/comment page with XSS payload in the base name; ParentPageNameTag renders the unencoded name into HTML body
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker needs ability to create wiki blog pages

findings.description: XSS in EditLinkTag - m_title and m_accesskey written into HTML attribute context without encoding
findings.code_snippet:
out.print("<a href=\""+m_wikiContext.getURL(WikiContext.EDIT,pageName, versionString)
         +"\" accesskey=\"" + m_accesskey + "\" title=\"" + m_title + "\">");
findings.reasoning: The `m_title` and `m_accesskey` attributes are set directly from JSP tag attributes (via `setTitle()` and `setAccesskey()`) and written into HTML attribute context without HTML encoding. If these tag attributes are dynamically populated from user-controlled data in a JSP template (e.g., via EL expressions or scriptlets that include page-name-derived data), an attacker could inject attribute-breaking characters like `"` followed by event handlers such as `onclick=alert(1)`. While these are typically static in templates, the lack of encoding represents a code-level vulnerability per secure coding standards.

evidence.notes: Compare with TabTag which applies TextUtil.replaceEntities() to both m_tabTitle and m_accesskey before storage. EditLinkTag and LinkToTag do not apply this sanitization.

exploit_payload.scenario: If JSP templates set the title/accesskey attributes from user-controlled data, an attacker can break out of the HTML attribute context and inject event handlers
exploit_payload.prerequisites: JSP template must dynamically set title or accesskey from user-controlled data
exploit_payload.category: Reflected XSS via HTML attribute injection

findings.description: XSS in LinkToTag - m_title and m_accesskey written into HTML attribute context without encoding
findings.code_snippet:
out.print("<a class=\""+linkclass+"\" href=\""+url+"\" accesskey=\"" 
              + m_accesskey + "\" title=\"" + m_title + "\">");
findings.reasoning: Same issue as EditLinkTag - `m_title` and `m_accesskey` are written into HTML attribute context without HTML encoding. The `setTitle()` and `setAccesskey()` methods assign values directly without any sanitization. Unlike TabTag (which uses `TextUtil.replaceEntities()` on similar attributes), LinkToTag does not encode these values. If dynamically populated from user-controlled data in JSP templates, an attacker could inject `"` followed by event handlers to break out of the attribute context.

evidence.notes: TabTag.java applies TextUtil.replaceEntities() to tabTitle, accesskey, and url. LinkToTag/EditLinkTag lack this protection.

exploit_payload.scenario: If JSP templates dynamically set title or accesskey from user-controlled data, an attacker can break out of the HTML attribute and inject event handlers
exploit_payload.prerequisites: JSP template must dynamically populate title or accesskey from user input
exploit_payload.category: Reflected XSS via HTML attribute injection

findings.description: CommentLinkTag - URL written to href attribute; getCommentURL builds URL from page name
findings.reasoning: The URL is constructed via `m_wikiContext.getURL(WikiContext.COMMENT, pageName)` which URL-encodes the page name as a query parameter. The output is written into an href attribute context. While HTML-encoding the URL would be best practice, the URL generation typically percent-encodes special characters, preventing direct HTML injection. The ANCHOR case writes the URL into a quoted href attribute - a `"` in the URL would be percent-encoded by the URL generator. This is a low-risk finding and likely a false positive for XSS, though a more thorough audit of `getURL()` encoding would be needed.

evidence.notes: The URL output in href context is lower risk. getURL() should URL-encode the page name parameter.

findings.description: DiffLinkTag - URL written to href attribute; URL constructed from page name and version numbers
findings.reasoning: The URL is constructed via `m_wikiContext.getURL(WikiContext.DIFF, pageName, "r1="+r1+"&r2="+r2)` where r1 and r2 are integer version numbers (safe, numeric only). The pageName is URL-encoded by `getURL()`. The URL is written into an href attribute context. This is low risk since version parameters are integers and the URL generator encodes the page name.

evidence.notes: Version numbers are integers - safe. Page name is URL-encoded by getURL(). Low XSS risk.

findings.description: PageVersionTag - outputs integer version number, no XSS risk
findings.reasoning: The output is `Integer.toString(version)` which is always a numeric string and cannot contain HTML special characters. This is a false positive - there is no XSS risk.

evidence.notes: Integer.toString() output is always safe - only contains digits.

findings.description: PageSizeTag - exception message written to HTML output without encoding
findings.reasoning: The `ProviderException.getMessage()` is written directly to HTML output without HTML encoding. If the exception message contains user-controlled data (e.g., a page name that triggered the provider error), this could lead to XSS. The risk depends on whether the underlying storage provider includes page names or other user-controlled strings in exception messages. This requires manual review to determine if provider exception messages can contain user-controlled HTML-significant characters.

evidence.notes: ProviderException messages may include page names or file paths that could contain HTML-special characters if an attacker chose a malicious page name.

findings.description: PageDateTag - date format output is safe, not user controlled
findings.reasoning: The output is a formatted date string from `SimpleDateFormat.format(Date)`. Date formatting produces numeric/symbol characters (e.g. "2023-01-15 10:30:00") that cannot contain HTML injection. The format pattern `m_format` comes from a JSP tag attribute (template author controlled), and even if an attacker could control it, date format patterns do not produce HTML-injectable output. This is a false positive.

evidence.notes: Date format output contains only date/time characters - no user-controlled strings that could contain HTML.

findings.description: UserNameTag - user name properly encoded with TextUtil.replaceEntities() before output
findings.reasoning: Line 59: The user name is encoded with `TextUtil.replaceEntities()` which replaces `&`, `<`, `>`, `"` with their HTML entities - this is properly sanitized. Lines 63-64: The fallback string comes from a resource bundle (i18n properties file), not from user input, so it's safe. Both output paths are safe from XSS. This is a false positive.

evidence.notes: TextUtil.replaceEntities() replaces &, <, >, " with HTML entities. The fallback is from a static resource bundle. Both paths are safe.

findings.description: BreadcrumbsTag line 181 - separator string printed, set by JSP template author not user input
findings.reasoning: The `m_separator` is set via `setSeparator()` from the JSP tag attribute `separator`. This is typically a static string set by template authors (default ", "), not directly from user HTTP request parameters. While a compromised template could set a malicious separator, this is a template author concern, not a user-controlled XSS vector. The CodeQL finding at line 181 refers to the separator output, which is effectively a template-controlled value.

evidence.notes: m_separator is a tag attribute set in JSP templates by authors, not from HTTP request parameters.

findings.description: PageSizeTag line 57 - Long.toString(size) is numeric only, no XSS risk
findings.reasoning: `Long.toString(size)` always produces a numeric string. This cannot contain HTML-special characters and poses no XSS risk. False positive.

evidence.notes: Long.toString() produces only digits and possibly a minus sign - never HTML-injectable.

findings.description: EditLinkTag line 138 - URL written in URL format case only, not HTML body injection
findings.reasoning: This is the URL-only format case outputting just a URL string. The `getURL()` method URL-encodes the page name. While writing a raw URL without HTML encoding isn't ideal (could break HTML if URL contains `&` or `"`), this represents low XSS risk as URL special characters are percent-encoded by the URL builder.

evidence.notes: URL output - page name is URL-encoded, low XSS risk in URL-only context.

findings.description: LinkToTag line 130 - URL written in URL format case only, low XSS risk
findings.reasoning: This is the URL-only format case. The URL is built by `m_wikiContext.getURL()` which URL-encodes the page name parameter. Writing the URL directly has low XSS risk as the URL builder handles special characters in the page name via percent-encoding.

evidence.notes: URL-only output case, page name is URL-encoded.

findings.description: CommentLinkTag line 86 - URL-only output case, low XSS risk
findings.reasoning: This is the URL-only format case. The URL is built by `m_wikiContext.getURL(WikiContext.COMMENT, pageName)` which URL-encodes the page name. Low XSS risk as URL builder handles special characters via percent-encoding.

evidence.notes: URL-only output - page name is URL-encoded by getURL().

findings.description: DiffLinkTag line 170 - URL-only output case, low XSS risk
findings.reasoning: This is the URL-only format case for DiffLinkTag. The URL is built from `getURL(WikiContext.DIFF, pageName, "r1="+r1+"&r2="+r2)` where r1/r2 are integer version numbers and pageName is URL-encoded by getURL(). No XSS risk.

evidence.notes: URL-only output, numeric params, URL-encoded page name.
